results.possibleIdentity: James Lennon (James Lennon, AIA) is a prominent architect and designer who specialized in healthcare architecture and the design of emergency departments. Active since the late 1960s and early 1970s, he emerged during the period when emergency medicine began to develop as a medical specialty. His work is defined by the principles of 'Evidence-Based Design' (EBD), which emphasizes the collaboration between architectural design, medical science, and technology to improve patient outcomes and operational efficiency. As the founder of Lennon Associates, he has received numerous awards, including the Wolfson Economics Prize in 2021 for 'The Smart ED' project, a reimagination of emergency departments using state-of-the-art technologies and human-centered design. His career aligns with the multidisciplinary ethos of organizations like Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) by fostering technical and scientific collaboration in the creative field of architecture.

Jim Lennon (James Lennon) is a professional commercial and art photographer based in Long Island, New York, who has been active for over 40 years, placing his early career in the late 1970s or early 1980s. His work primarily focuses on the documentation of the arts, including live stage performances by orchestras, ballet companies, and modern dance troupes, as well as the documentation of visual art exhibits. He has been recognized with awards from organizations such as Kodak and the Association of the Graphic Arts. In 2021, he published a book titled 'The Shoreline' which features his fine art photography.

results.possibleIdentity: Michael Jon "Mickey" Dvortcsak (1938–2019) was an American abstract artist and educator based in California. He began his artistic career in the 1960s as a student at the University of California, Santa Barbara (UCSB), where he studied under Howard Warshaw and Rico Lebrun, eventually earning his MFA in 1968. He was actively involved in the intellectual movement bridging art, science, and technology during the late 1960s; notably, he authored the essay "The Artist/Scientist in the Twentieth Century," published in the Harvard Art Review in 1967. This work, alongside his experimentation with new materials and biomorphic, molecular substructured imagery, aligns him with the era and mission of the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) organization. Dvortcsak taught at UCSB until the mid-1970s before pursuing painting full-time. His work often utilized a distinctive oil and wax technique and transitioned from early reductive abstractions to more figurative "Museum Suites" in his later years. His art is held in numerous permanent collections, including the Museum of Contemporary Art (MOCA) in Los Angeles, the Walker Art Center in Minneapolis, and the Santa Barbara Museum of Art. He was a lifelong friend and former roommate of the sculptor Richard Serra.
